1: wayyissaʾeni ruach — "the Spirit lifted me up" — the verb nasaʾ (lift/carry) with ruach as subject is Ezekiel's standard transport-formula (also 3:12, 14; 8:3; 43:5). Ruach here cannot be translated "wind" as in natural contexts — the prophetic transport is the Spirit's agency. The gate facing east where Ezekiel lands is the same gate through which YHWH's glory will return in 43:1-4.
2: The two men and their twenty-five colleagues — likely military and civic leaders of Jerusalem — are singled out by name (Jaazaniah and Pelatiah). The naming in prophetic vision is the OT equivalent of the public accusation; these are not symbolic figures but real officials whose counsel is condemned as contrary to YHWH's purposes.
3: lo beqarov livnot batim — "it is not near to build houses" — the leaders' slogan denies the urgency of judgment. The implied meaning is: "we are safe in Jerusalem (the pot); the exile crowd is already gone." The pot metaphor (sir, cooking pot) is their image of security, which Ezekiel inverts: the pot is the place of cooking, not protection.
4: The divine command to prophesy (hinave, imperative piel of nava) against them is addressed to Ezekiel while still in the vision-state. The imperative is direct and immediate — piel aspect emphasizes the declared/prophetic nature of the speech act, distinguishing it from ordinary speech.
5: vatippol ʿalai ruach YHWH — "the Spirit of YHWH fell on me" — the verb nafal (fall) describes the sudden, overwhelming onset of prophetic inspiration. The same vocabulary appears for the Spirit falling on Saul (1 Sam 10:10) and Samson (Judg 14:6). The divine speech that follows ("thus says YHWH") is authorized by this Spirit-falling.
6: The accusation — you have multiplied your slain in this city — uses the perfect hirbatem (you have multiplied) as an accomplished fact. The fill-the-streets-with-corpses imagery (malletem ḥutsoteha ḥalal) is the judicial evidence that makes the leaders guilty of blood.
7: ʾasher natattem betokhah hem habasar vehi hasir vaʾatem hotsenu mitokhah — the wordplay reverses the leaders' slogan: yes, the city is a pot and you are in it — but the meat in the pot is the slain, not you; and you will be removed from it (the exile that is coming is not protection but expulsion). The grammatical stress on hem (they, emphatic) and vaʾatem (but you) creates a sharp contrast.
8: The fear they claim not to have (haḥerev yeratem) is what they will face: they feared the sword of enemies and so formed foreign alliances; YHWH will bring on them the very sword they tried to avoid. The irony of self-fulfilling judgment through policy choices is the structural point of vv7-8.
9: vehoseti etchem mitokha venatati etchem beyad zarim — the two-step judgment: extraction (hoseti, hiphil of yatsa, I will bring out) from the city and delivery (natati beyad zarim, I will give into the hand of strangers). The same verb pair (yatsa + natan beyad) appears throughout Judges and Kings for YHWH delivering Israel to enemies.
10: The border of Israel as the place of judgment — ʿal gevul Yisraʾel eshpet etchem — specifies the location: the frontier where Nebuchadnezzar caught and judged Zedekiah (2 Kgs 25:6-7; Jer 52:9-10). The prophecy is precise enough to be a prediction of an historical event, and precisely fulfilled.
11: The repeated declaration — hi lo tihyeh lachem lesir vaʾatem lo tihyu vasar betokhah — "this city will not be a pot for you and you will not be flesh within it" — is the definitive reversal of the leaders' slogan. The verb tihyeh/tihyu (imperfect of hayah) is a firm future declaration, not a possibility.
12: uveḥuqqot haggoyim asher sevivoteikhem ʿasitem — "you acted according to the statutes of the nations around you" — the word ḥuqqot (statutes/ordinances) used for pagan practices inverts the covenant formula: YHWH's ḥuqqim (statutes) were the law Israel was to follow; using ḥuqqot haggoyim condemns them for having a rival statutory code.
13: The death of Pelatiah (vayamat Pelatiah ven Benayahu) during the prophetic speech — while Ezekiel is still in the vision — is a sign-act death. Ezekiel's response (falling on his face, crying out) matches his response to the glory-vision in ch 1. The death within the vision corresponds to what will happen in history.
14: The transition to the consolation oracle — announced by the formula vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor (the word of YHWH came to me saying) — marks a new divine speech unit. The formula is the standard Ezekielian messenger-speech opener and signals a shift in tone.
15: The insulting slogan of the Jerusalem residents — riḥaqu meʿal YHWH lahem nittenah haʾarets lemorah — "they have gone far from YHWH; to us the land is given as a possession" — treats proximity to Jerusalem as proximity to YHWH. The perfect riḥaqu (they have gone far) is their assessment of the exiles; the divine response inverts it completely in what follows.
16: vehiʾi lahem lemigdash meʿat baʾaratsot asher baʾu sham — "I have been to them a little sanctuary in the countries where they have gone" — migdash meʿat (little/temporary sanctuary) is one of Ezekiel's most profound coinages. YHWH himself is the sanctuary in exile, replacing the temple as the place of divine presence. The term meʿat (a little, of minor extent) may be the divine humility of accompanying the exiles into Babylon.
17: The gathering-from-peoples promise — veqibbatsti etchem mehaʿamim veʾasafti etchem mehaʾaratsot — uses the same verb pair (qabats + ʾasaf, gather + collect) that Deuteronomy 30:3-4 uses for the eschatological regathering. The two verbs reinforce each other: qabats is the general term; ʾasaf (collect/harvest) adds the nuance of gathering what was scattered.
18: The promise of their cultic cleansing — vehesiru kol-shiqqutseyha ukhol-toʿavoteha mimennah — "they will remove all its detestable things and all its abominations from it" — uses shiqqutsim (detestable things, often idols) and toʿavot (abominations). Both terms are strong cultic-pollution vocabulary. The imperfect vehesiru is part of the sequential future narrative that follows the gathering promise.
19: venatati lahem lev ʾeḥad veruach ḥadashah etten beqirbam vehashirothi et-lev haʾeven mibesaram venatati lahem lev basar — the new-heart/new-spirit promise anticipates 36:26 almost word for word. Key terms: lev ʾeḥad (one heart — undivided loyalty, unlike the divided heart of v21); ruach ḥadashah (new spirit — not just reformed behavior but a new animating principle); lev haʾeven (heart of stone — the unresponsive covenant heart); lev basar (heart of flesh — responsive, feeling, alive to YHWH's word).
20: The covenant-formula conclusion — vehayu li leʿam vaʾani ʾehyeh lahem leʾlohim — "they shall be my people and I will be their God" — is the Sinaitic covenant formula in its simplest form (also Jer 31:33). The imperfects express ongoing covenant relationship. The formula occurs at the climax of restoration promises throughout the prophets.
21: The contrasting judgment clause — veʾel-lev shiqqutseyhem vetoʿavoteyhem libam holeikh — "as for those whose heart goes after their detestable things and their abominations" — the heart-going-after (holeikh, participle of halakh) the idols is the opposite of the one-heart promised in v19. The participial form describes an ongoing disposition.
22: The departure of the kavod (glory) — vayyissaʾu hakheruvim ʾet-kanfeyhem — the cherubim lifting their wings in preparation for departure is the signal that the kavod YHWH is about to move. The same verb nasaʾ (lift) used for Ezekiel being carried is used for the wings. The symmetry is deliberate: the prophet and the glory move together.
23: The kavod YHWH pausing ʿal-hahar ʾasher miqqedem laʿir — "on the mountain east of the city" — the Mount of Olives. This is the last resting place of the divine glory before it leaves for Babylon (implied in chs 10-11). The mountain east of the city is where the glory will return in 43:1-4 and where Zechariah 14:4 locates the eschatological theophany.
24: The Spirit-transport home — veruach nesaʾtni vateviʾeni khaldeʾyma — the same verb (nasaʾ) for the return trip. The whole vision journey is framed by Spirit-transport. The phrase bemar'eh beruach (in the vision by the Spirit) clarifies that the journey was visionary, not physical — an important marker in a book full of border-crossing between vision and reality.
25: The report to the exiles — vaʾadabber ʾel-haggolah ʾet kol-divrei YHWH — Ezekiel as messenger to the community. The term haggolah (the exile community/the deportees) is Ezekiel's standard designation for his audience. The act of speaking "all the words of YHWH" affirms the completeness of the prophetic report — nothing withheld from the community.
